coredata.dc:title: Optimized spacing of active sites in Ni-Mo catalysts enhances ethanol production in syngas conversion

coredata.dc:description: The direct conversion of syngas to ethanol is highly attractive but remains challenging due to low CO conversion and poor EtOH/ROH. We investigated the conversion of syngas over a KNiMo2C catalyst modified with carbon dots (CDs) derived from Ligustrum lucidum leaves. On the Ni0.54Mo-A catalyst, a CO conversion of 43.4 % was achieved, with oxygenates selectivity and EtOH/ROH，reaching 48.2 % and 63.7 %, the space-time yield of ethanol was 132.5 mg/gcat/h. Comprehensive characterization revealed that CDs regulate the distance between Ni-Mo active sites, preventing excessive proximity and maintaining an optimal separation. This adjustment significantly enhances EtOH/ROH by modulating the probability of carbon chain growth. A potential new reaction pathway for directly converting syngas to ethanol is proposed, The C-C coupling of CHx* with *COOH or *CO2 is identified as a key step in carbon chain growth. This work provides a new catalytic pathway for the syngas to ethanol industry.

head.ce:title: Optimized spacing of active sites in Ni-Mo catalysts enhances ethanol production in syngas conversion

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The direct conversion of syngas to ethanol is highly attractive but remains challenging due to low CO conversion and poor EtOH/ROH. We investigated the conversion of syngas over a KNiMo2C catalyst modified with carbon dots (CDs) derived from Ligustrum lucidum leaves. On the Ni0.54Mo-A catalyst, a CO conversion of 43.4 % was achieved, with oxygenates selectivity and EtOH/ROH，reaching 48.2 % and 63.7 %, the space-time yield of ethanol was 132.5 mg/gcat/h. Comprehensive characterization revealed that CDs regulate the distance between Ni-Mo active sites, preventing excessive proximity and maintaining an optimal separation. This adjustment significantly enhances EtOH/ROH by modulating the probability of carbon chain growth. A potential new reaction pathway for directly converting syngas to ethanol is proposed, The C-C coupling of CHx* with *COOH or *CO2 is identified as a key step in carbon chain growth. This work provides a new catalytic pathway for the syngas to ethanol industry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the greenhouse effect [1,2] and energy crisis have increasingly impacted human society, and the direct catalytic conversion of syngas to ethanol (STE) has attracted widespread attention. CO2 [3,4] and green hydrogen can generate syngas through the reverse water-gas shift reaction (RWGS), and the STE is a green pathway to effectively mitigate the greenhouse effect and energy crisis. Ethanol is an important transportation fuel [5] and a fundamental chemical feedstock, however, 90 % of ethanol production each year comes from grain fermentation [6], which is an impractical use of large quantities of food. The STE has an enticing industrial outlook but currently suffers from low yields, primarily due to the lack of high-performance catalysts.

To date, catalysts used for directly converting syngas to ethanol and C2+OH (alcohols with carbon numbers greater than or equal to 2) can be broadly classified into four categories. The first category comprises modified methanol catalysts (Cu [7,8], Cr [9]), while the second category includes modified Fischer-Tropsch catalysts (CuFe [10,11], CuCo [12,13], and CuNi[14]). Typically, modified methanol catalysts exhibit high selectivity towards methanol, whereas modified Fischer-Tropsch catalysts show higher selectivity towards hydrocarbons. The third category involves noble metal catalysts (Rh), which exhibit high selectivity for ethanol and C2+OH. However, their economic feasibility is poor, making large-scale industrial applications unlikely. The fourth category consists of Mo-based catalysts (MoS2 [15], Mo2C [16], MoOx [17,18], etc.), which exhibit multiple adjustable oxidation states, excellent sulfur resistance, and anti-coking properties, making them highly promising for the direct conversion of syngas to C2+OH. The product mixture obtained from the direct conversion of syngas is complex, and the further separation and purification of methanol and C2+OH require high energy consumption. Therefore, significantly enhancing the ethanol yield could substantially reduce energy consumption and improve economic efficiency.

It is widely acknowledged that catalysts for directly converting syngas to ethanol and C2+OH must involve the synergistic effect of multiple active sites. Specifically, CO dissociative adsorption is required to produce CHx*, alongside non-dissociative CO adsorption to generate CO* (adsorbed CO), enabling the insertion of CO* into CHx*[19,20] and facilitating carbon chain growth to form C2+OH. Alkaline promoters such as K enhance the non-dissociative adsorption capacity of CO in Mo-based catalysts, thereby improving the overall alcohol selectivity of the products [21]. Transition metal promoters like Ni primarily improve the dissociative adsorption capacity of CO, thus enhancing the selectivity for C2+OH [22]. Researchers have indicated that atomic-level close contact between transition metal-Mo active sites can enhance their synergistic interaction, thereby improving carbon chain growth and enhancing ethanol and C2+OH selectivity. Asundi et al. [23] found that increasing oxygenate yield does not depend on the formation of specific mixed metal carbide phases but rather on the closely contacted active sites of Co and Mo on the catalyst surface. Hao et al. [24] suggested that the interaction between Ni and Mo increases the electron density of Mo species, promoting non-dissociative CO adsorption and insertion. Li et al.[25] reached similar conclusions, indicating that the interaction between La and Ni facilitates close contact between Ni and the MoS2 matrix, favoring the formation of C2+OH. Wang et al.[26] constructed closely contacted Ni-Co alloys, which enhanced the distribution of alcohols, particularly ethanol. The close atomic-level contact between transition metals and Mo active sites can improve the transfer efficiency of active species and C2+OH selectivity. While excessive proximity may not be beneficial for improving ethanol selectivity and distribution. Therefore, constructing appropriate distance between transition metal and Mo active sites is crucial for the design of catalysts for the direct conversion of syngas to ethanol.

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional materials typically smaller than 10 nm [27,28]. They offer various advantages, including simple synthesis methods, low toxicity, high electrical conductivity and photoluminescence. The surface of carbon dots contains numerous functional groups, such as -NH2, -COOH, and -OH [29]. These provide strong anchoring sites for transition metal atoms, effectively preventing thermal aggregation of metal particles during pyrolysis, thus enabling the preparation of various transition metal-loaded carbon catalysts [30]. Compared to conventional carbon supports like activated carbon, these catalysts exhibit better dispersion of transition metal active sites and possess strong electron transfer capabilities. Currently, carbon dots are primarily applied in photocatalysis and electrocatalysis, but their use in traditional thermal catalysis remains limited.

In this study, we synthesized a series of catalysts using freeze-drying and temperature-programmed reduction methods. By adjusting the presence of CDs and the mixing sequence of the Mo source, Ni source, and CDs solution (Ni0.54Mo-TS, Ni0.54Mo-S, Ni0.54Mo-A, and Ni0.54Mo-L represent the shortest, short, appropriate, and long distances between Ni -Mo active sites), the distance between the Ni-Mo active sites can be regulated to enhance ethanol selectivity and alcohol distribution. The ethanol selectivity of the Ni0.54Mo-A catalyst reached 26.2 %, with an impressive alcohol distribution of 63.7 %, and a space-time yield (STY) of ethanol of 132.5 mg/gcat/h. Additionally, the Ni0.54Mo-TS catalyst achieved a C2+OH distribution of 93.9 %. To better analyze the structure-performance relationship and catalytic mechanism, various characterization techniques were employed, including ICP-OES, BET, SEM, XRD, XPS, TEM, CO/H2/CO2/NH3-TPD，H2-TPR and in-situ infrared spectroscopy (In situ DRIFTS). In situ, DRIFTS revealed that there might be multiple pathways for carbon chain growth during the conversion of syngas to ethanol or C2+OH. Our current work significantly improves the distribution of ethanol and substantially increases the space-time yield of ethanol, while markedly suppressing methanol formation. This study provides a method to enhance ethanol selectivity by adjusting the distance between Ni-Mo active sites.

Carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized using the classical hydrothermal method，as shown in Figure S1. In brief, 15 g of freshly washed Privet leaves were cut into small pieces and placed into a 200 mL hydrothermal reactor. Then, 120 mL of deionized water was added, and the mixture was heated at 180 °C for 8 h in an oven. The resultant brownish-clear solution was filtered under vacuum, followed by freeze-drying to obtain CDs. The brownish-clear solution was dialyzed using a MW500 dialysis bag for 24 h, with the deionized water being replaced every 6 h. The dialyzed dCDs were then obtained by freeze-drying.

The catalyst was prepared using the conventional temperature-programmed reduction method. Specifically, 3 g of CDs, 2.76 g of (NH4)6Mo7O24∙4 H2O, and a certain amount of Ni(CH3COO)2∙4 H2O were dissolved separately in 100 mL, 60 mL, and 40 mL of deionized water, respectively, to obtain solutions labeled as solutions a, b, and c. After mixing the solutions in a specific sequence was freeze-dried and ground into powder using an agate mortar. The powder was then placed into a tubular furnace, followed by introducing a 10 % H2/N2 mixed gas. The temperature was ramped up to 720°C at a rate of 5 °C/min and held for 3 hours. After cooling to 135°C, CO2 gas was introduced, and the mixture was kept at this temperature for 2 hours before being naturally cooled to room temperature to yield a black solid powder. Subsequently, 0.147 g of K2CO3 was dissolved in an appropriate amount of deionized water, and the resulting solution was impregnated onto the aforementioned black powder solid. The impregnated material was then dried at 110°C for 12 h in an oven to obtain the final catalyst product.

Under stirring conditions, solutions a and b were first mixed and stirred for 30 seconds, followed by adding solution c to the mixed solution. The resulting catalysts were named Ni0.14Mo-A, Ni0.27Mo-A, Ni0.54Mo-A, and Ni0.82Mo-A, respectively. Similarly, when solutions b and c were mixed first, followed by adding solution a, the catalyst Ni0.54Mo-S was obtained. When solutions a and c were mixed first, followed by adding solution “b”, the catalyst Ni0.54Mo-B was obtained. Solution “a” was divided into two equal parts, each mixed with solutions b and c. The two mixed solutions were then combined to obtain the catalyst Ni0.54Mo-C. In NixMo-Y, x represents the molar ratio of Ni to Mo, and Y represents the different mixing methods.

Catalysts without solution c were named Mo-CDs, those without solution b were named Ni-CDs, and derivatives obtained solely by calcining CDs were named C-CDs. Catalysts prepared using dialyzed CDs were named Ni0.54Mo-dCD, the catalyst prepared without CDs and used glucose as the carbon source was named Ni0.54Mo-TS, the catalyst with a physical mixture of Ni-CDs and Mo-CDs is called Ni0.54Mo-L, and the catalyst prepared solely using (NH4)6Mo7O24∙4 H2O and C6H12O6 were named Mo2C.

To compare the effects of different carbon sources, we prepared catalysts using activated carbon as the carbon source via the classical impregnation method and temperature-programmed reduction [31], naming the catalyst Ni0.54Mo-Ac. Additionally, we used a mixed gas (15 % CH4 - 85 % H2) as the carbon source and employed the classical temperature-programmed reduction method [32] to prepare another catalyst, named Ni0.54Mo-Gas. The Ni/Mo molar ratio used is consistent with that of Ni0.54Mo-A.
The details of catalyst characterizations and evaluation can be seen in the Supporting Information.

To enhance the ethanol selectivity from STE on Mo-based catalysts, we employed a strategy of leaf-derived CDs modification on KNiMo2C catalysts. By regulating the spatial distance between Ni-Mo active sites and altering the electronic properties and microenvironment of Mo sites, we adjusted the appropriate probability of carbon chain growth to improve ethanol and C2+OH selectivity. Initially, we prepared precursor catalysts using the hydrothermal method [33] and temperature-programmed reduction methods [31]. Subsequently, we impregnated K2CO3 to synthesize a series of catalysts with different Ni/Mo molar ratios, denoted as NixMo, where x represents the atomic molar ratio of Ni to Mo. Additionally, the different prepared plans can adjust the distance between Ni-Mo active sites, mainly in different precursor solution mixing sequences (Mo source, Ni source, and CDs). Ni0.54Mo-TS, Ni0.54Mo-S, Ni0.54Mo-A, and Ni0.54Mo-L represent the shortest, short, appropriate, and long distances between Ni -Mo active sites, respectively. The detailed catalyst preparation process is provided in the supporting information. Subsequently, we further investigated the performance of the catalysts in catalyzing the conversion of syngas to alcohols under conditions of 360 °C, 8.0 MPa, 5000 mL/gcat/h, and H2/CO = 1, as shown in Tables S1-S2.

We first examined the effect of the Ni/Mo molar ratio on catalytic performance, under conditions of 360 °C, 8.0 MPa, 5000 mL/gcat/h, and H2/CO = 1, as shown in Fig. 1 and Table S1. When Ni was not introduced (Mo-CDs), the CO conversion was 35.2 %, with a hydrocarbon selectivity of 90.0 %, ethanol selectivity of only 3.1 %, and a C2+OH distribution of 81.1 %. Conversely, the CO conversion of Ni-CDs was extremely low at only 5.4 %, with a hydrocarbon selectivity reaching 100 %. The combination of Ni and Mo improve the catalytic performance. As the Ni/Mo molar ratio increased in NixMo catalysts, the CO conversion and hydrocarbon selectivity decreased from (65.5 %, 61.4 %) to (25.7 %, 45.2 %), respectively, while the corresponding oxygenated compound (mainly alcohols) selectivity increased from 38.6 % to 54.8 %, as depicted in Fig. 1A. In term of alcohol distribution, ethanol and propanol were the main alcohol products, with ethanol in alcohols distribution (EtOH/ROH) showing the “volcanic” change trend with the rise of the Ni/Mo molar ratio, whereas C3+OH/ROH exhibited the opposite trend. Among them, the Ni0.54Mo-A catalyst exhibited the highest ethanol selectivity at 26.2 %, and EtOH/ROH accounted for an impressive 63.7 %. It is worth mentioning that MeOH/ROH remained at a low level (<10 %), as shown in Fig. 1B, demonstrating that it displayed the better ability of C-C coupling. As far as space-time yields of alcohols (STYROH) was concerned, as the Ni/Mo molar ratio increased, both STYROH and STYC2+OH gradually decreased. However, the STYEtOH displays the “volcanic” change trend, with the highest STYEtOH of 145.9 mg/gcat/h achieved by Ni0.27Mo-A, as shown in Fig. 1C. Furthermore, the probability of carbon chain growth (α) showed the inverted “volcanic” trend, reaching a minimum of 0.249 on Ni0.54Mo-A., as depicted in Figure S3A. In the hydrocarbon distribution, the methane distribution remained almost unchanged, while the distributions of ethylene and propylene exhibited an increasing trend, as shown in Figure S2 and Table S2.

To investigate the impact of the distance between Ni and Mo active sites on catalytic performance, we synthesized Ni0.54Mo-TS, Ni0.54Mo-S, Ni0.54Mo-A, and Ni0.54Mo-L with a Ni/Mo molar ratio of 0.54. The CO conversion for these catalysts were found to decrease sequentially, at 81.5 %, 61.1 %, 43.2 %, and 42.0 %, respectively. Both Ni0.54Mo-TS and Ni0.54Mo-L exhibited higher total hydrocarbon selectivity, at 69.2 % and 65.4 %, respectively, while Ni0.54Mo-A and Ni0.54Mo-S displayed higher total oxygenate selectivity, at 48.2 % and 45.4 %. Notably, the methane selectivity for Ni0.54Mo-TS was 42.3 %, almost double that of Ni0.54Mo-A, as shown in Fig. 1D. As the increase of the distance, the selectivity for ethanol exhibited the “volcanic” change trend, with Ni0.54Mo-A achieving the highest ethanol selectivity at 26.2 %, more than twice that of Ni0.54Mo-L. The distribution of ethanol within the total alcohols also followed this trend, with Ni0.54Mo-A reaching an impressive 63.7 %, which is exceptional given the CO conversion of approximately 45 %. The long distance between Ni and Mo active sites obtained the lowest C2+OH distribution. Ni0.54Mo-TS achieved a remarkable C2+OH distribution of 93.9 %, as illustrated in Fig. 1E.

STYROH and STYC2+OH for Ni0.54Mo-TS, Ni0.54Mo-S, Ni0.54Mo-A, and Ni0.54Mo-L showed a decreasing trend, whereas the STYEtOH initially increased and then decreased, with Ni0.54Mo-A having the highest STYEtOH at 132.5 mg/gcat/h. In comparison, the STYEtOH for Ni0.54Mo-TS and Ni0.54Mo-L were 99.4 mg/gcat/h and 62.8 mg/gcat/h, respectively. Additionally, Ni0.54Mo-S exhibited the highest total STY at 351.6 mg/gcat/h, as depicted in Fig. 1F. The carbon chain growth probabilities of the alcohol products for Ni0.54Mo-TS and Ni0.54Mo-L were 0.386 and 0.370, respectively, which were significantly higher than the 0.249 observed for Ni0.54Mo-A, as shown in Figure S3B. Those demonstrated that Ni0.54Mo-A displayed the poor carbon chain growth probabilities of the alcohol products. The strong carbon chain growth probability is not unfavorable for obtaining high ethanol distribution. In summary, the appropriate distance achieved better ethanol selectivity, distribution and STYEtOH.

To explore the impact of reaction conditions on the catalytic performance of Ni0.54Mo-A, we studied three variables: space velocity, reaction temperature and pressure. The detailed results are shown in Figures S4, S5 and Tables S3 and S4. As the space velocity decreases from 8000 to 2000 mL/gcat/h, the CO conversion rate increases, while the selectivity of oxygenates remains relatively stable, with a slight increase at space velocities of 8000 mL/gcat/h and 2000 mL/gcat/h. Under a space velocity of 5000 mL/gcat/h, ethanol selectivity and alcohol distribution are significantly higher than the other two conditions. Besides, the time-space yields of total organics, alcohols, and C2+OH decrease, while the STYEtOH exhibits “volcanic” changes. With a decrease in reaction temperature, CO conversion declines, while the selectivity of total oxygenates rises. At 360 °C, ethanol selectivity and alcohol distribution outperform the other two conditions. Moreover, the time-space yields of total oxygenate, alcohols, and C2+OH slightly decrease, but the STYEtOH display “volcanic” trend. As reaction pressure decreases from 8 MPa to 3 MPa, CO conversion drops significantly, but the selectivity of hydrocarbons and oxygenates shows minimal variation. Under 8 MPa, ethanol shows optimal alcohol distribution and selectivity. The time-space yields of total organics, alcohols, C2+OH, and ethanol all exhibit a downward trend. Under the current laboratory conditions, the reaction conditions of 360°C, 8 MPa, and a space velocity of 5000 h⁻¹ are likely the most favorable for this reaction.

Compared the effects of different carbon sources on catalytic performance, we prepared catalysts using activated carbon as the support (Ni0.54Mo-Ac) and gas-phase carburization (Ni0.54Mo-Gas), as shown in Figures S6, S7, and Tables S5 and S6. The oxygenated organic compounds selectivity of Ni0.54Mo-Ac and Ni0.54Mo-Gas are 17.7 % and 30.8 %, respectively, significantly lower than that of the Ni0.54Mo-A catalyst (48.2 %). Ethanol selectivity follows a similar trend. In terms of the ethanol distribution, the Ni0.54Mo-Ac (32.5 %) and Ni0.54Mo-Gas (36.4 %) catalysts are noticeably lower than that of Ni0.54Mo-A (63.7 %). However, it is noteworthy that the C2+OH distribution of Ni0.54Mo-Gas catalyst reaches 92.1 %, demonstrating that the Ni0.54Mo-Gas catalyst possess the strong carbon chain growth capability. In summary, the catalytic performance of Ni0.54Mo-A is significantly superior to that of both Ni0.54Mo-Ac and Ni0.54Mo-Gas.

Compared with the performance of previous catalysts, this study lists several representative catalysts for the conversion of syngas to ethanol as reported in the literature, as shown in Fig. 2 and Table S15. Previous studies have shown a certain trade-off effect between CO conversion and ethanol distribution, resulting in relatively low STY of ethanol. In contrast, the Ni0.54Mo-A catalyst in this study significantly improved CO conversion rates while achieving an ethanol distribution of 63.7 %, thereby markedly enhancing the STYEtOH. This performance is notably superior to that of the other widely studied Mo-based catalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure characterization, chemical and electronic properties of the catalysts
ce:section.ce:para:
The catalyst was characterized using XRD, XPS, TEM, ICP-OES, N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, and SEM to investigate its elemental composition, morphology, phase composition, structural properties, and electronic characteristics. The characterization results of the catalyst are shown in
Figs. 3, 4, S9-S18, and Tables S7-S13. The prepared CDs exhibit a yellowish-brown, layered structure with an average particle size of approximately 14.2 nm. The larger particle size may be attributed to the top-down method used, derived from the decomposition of biomass. ICP-OES was performed on Ni0.14Mo-A, Ni0.27Mo-A, Ni0.54Mo-A, and Ni0.82Mo-A. It was observed that the Mo content slightly decreased with an increase in Ni content, while the K content decreased significantly, as shown in Table S7. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) of Ni0.54Mo-A revealed that the catalyst exhibited a shape similar to square shaped layered stone, as depicted in Figure S11. N2 adsorption-desorption of the catalyst showed that the specific surface area of CDs after calcination (C-CDs) was 128.1 m2/g. With an increase in the Ni/Mo molar ratio, the specific surface area of the catalyst increased and then decreased. As illustrated in Figure S9 and summarized in Table S8, among them, Ni0.27Mo-A exhibited the highest specific surface area at 157.1 m2/g, while Ni0.54Mo-S had a smaller specific surface area of 75.7 m2/g. The overall catalyst exhibited a rich pore structure containing numerous mesoporous structures. It is interesting that although Ni0.54Mo-TS has a specific surface area of only 21.5 m²/g, it turns out to be the catalyst with the highest CO conversion rate. In generally, a larger specific surface area generally exposes more active sites, thereby enhancing CO conversion. However, the adjustment of the Ni-Mo active site distance may play a more significant role in both CO conversion and ethanol selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The distance between Ni-Mo active sites
ce:section.ce:para: All catalysts were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), and the results are shown in Figs. 3, S7 and S13. When no Ni source was introduced, the Mo2C peak in Mo-CDs was relatively small, as shown in Figure S7A. However, after introducing the Ni source, the Mo2C peak in Ni0.54Mo-A was significantly higher, as shown in Fig. 3A. This indicates that Mo2C formation is challenging with only the Mo source and carbon dots, and that Ni plays a critical role in the Mo2C formation process. When the catalyst was prepared by mixing only Mo source and glucose followed by calcination (Fig. 3A), the XRD peaks appeared at positions such as 34.36°, 37.98°, 39.14°, and 52.14°, which matched well with the Mo2C PDF card JCPDS#01–079–0744. After mixing Ni source and CDs solution followed by calcination to obtain the Ni-CDs catalyst, the XRD peaks appeared at 44.37°, 51.60°, and 76.08°, which matched well with the Ni PDF card JCPDS#01–087–0712. Comparing the XRD patterns of Ni0.54Mo-A, Ni0.54Mo-S, and Ni0.54Mo-TS, the diffraction peaks of Mo2C shifted towards larger angles, and the corresponding Ni diffraction peaks shifted towards smaller angles. Ni0.54Mo-L catalyst composed of physically mixed Ni-CDs and Mo-CDs shows Ni diffraction peaks that align well with those specified in JCPDS#01–087–0712. When the Mo source solution is mixed with the CDs solution before adding the Ni source solution, the resulting catalyst Ni0.54Mo-A exhibits the smallest shift angle δ2 of the Ni diffraction peak.

Conversely, when the Mo source solution is mixed with the Ni source solution before adding the CDs, the catalyst Ni0.54Mo-S shows a large shift angle δ4 of the Ni diffraction peak compared to δ2 of Ni0.54Mo-A. For the catalyst Ni0.54Mo-TS prepared without adding CDs, but only with the mixing of Mo source and Ni source solutions, the Ni diffraction peak shift angle δ6 is the largest. Simultaneously, the diffraction peaks of Mo2C also undergo corresponding changes, shifting towards larger angles [38–40]. Notably, when the Mo source solution is mixed with the CDs solution first, the Mo2C diffraction angle exhibits the maximum shift. These findings suggest that partial Mo atoms are incorporated into the Ni lattice, leading to an expansion of the Ni lattice spacing and a corresponding decrease in the Mo2C lattice spacing, as evidenced by the XRD peak shifts towards smaller and larger angles, respectively. However, the presence of CDs appears to weaken this effect. CDs is more prone to binding with the Mo source, attenuating the close intermixing effect between Mo and Ni atoms and increasing the distance between Ni-Mo active sites. Therefore, in the catalysts Ni0.54Mo-TS, Ni0.54Mo-S, Ni0.54Mo-A, and Ni0.54Mo-L, the distance between Ni-Mo active sites likely increase sequentially. Similar conclusions can be drawn by comparing the XRD patterns of catalysts with different Ni/Mo molar ratios and different solution mixing sequences, as shown in Figure S12. Additionally, we conducted XRD on the used catalysts Ni0.54Mo-TS, Ni0.54Mo-S, Ni0.54Mo-A, and Ni0.54Mo-L, as shown in Figure S21. The XRD pattern of Ni0.54Mo-TS-spent clearly exhibits a new alloy phase, MoNi4, which was not observed in the other three used catalysts. This finding further supports the notion that the Ni-Mo active site distance in the Ni0.54Mo-TS catalyst is likely the closest among the four catalysts.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterization results are shown in Figs. 3D, E, S14, and Tables S10 and S11. Comparing the XPS spectra of catalysts with different Ni/Mo molar ratios, the Mo3d5/2 binding energies of Ni0.14Mo-A, Ni0.27Mo-A, Ni0.54Mo-A, and Ni0.82Mo-A are assigned to Mo2+ at 229.00 eV, 228.80 eV [41], 228.74 eV [42], and 228.5 eV [43], respectively. Similarly, Mo3d5/2 binding energies around 233.04 eV [43], 234.15 eV [44], and 235.47 eV [45] are attributed to Mo4+, Mo5+, and Mo6+, respectively. With the Ni/Mo molar ratio increase, the Mo3d5/2 binding energy gradually shifts towards lower binding energies. This indicates that with an increase in Ni content, more Ni atoms transfer electrons to Mo atoms, decreasing the Mo3d5/2 binding energy. This suggests that Ni active sites and Mo active sites are in close contact, which is consistent with the conclusions drawn from the XRD analysis. Comparision of the Mo3d XPS spectra of Ni0.54Mo-A, and Ni0.54Mo-S shows that the binding energy of Ni0.54Mo-A overall shifts towards higher binding energies compared to the other three. Analysis indicates that the Ni0.54Mo-A catalyst in which the Mo source is mixed with the CDs solution first, exhibits a wide variety of Mo oxidation states, including Mo2+, Mo4+, Mo5+, and Mo6+. In contrast, the other three catalysts only show Mo2+ and Mo6+. This suggests that CDs significantly influence the oxidation states of surface Mo species in the catalyst, enriching the surface Mo oxidation states. The Mo3d5/2 binding energy of Mo2+ and Mo6+ in Ni0.54Mo-S is 228.40 eV and 233.1 eV, significantly lower than the Mo3d5/2 binding energy of Mo2+ in Ni0.54Mo-A (228.74 eV and 235.4 eV). Correspondingly, the binding energies of Ni2+ and Ni3+ in Ni2p3/2 of Ni0.54Mo-S are 856.13 eV [46] and 856.61 eV [47], respectively, which are notably higher than those in Ni0.54Mo-A (855.94 eV [48] and 856.43 eV [49]). This indicates that in the catalyst Ni0.54Mo-S, more electrons may transfer from Ni to Mo. Consequently, the distance between Ni-Mo active sites in the catalyst Ni0.54Mo-S may be smaller than that in the catalyst Ni0.54Mo-A, supporting the conclusions drawn from XRD analysis.

To further confirm the states of Ni-Mo active sites in Ni0.54Mo-A and Ni0.54Mo-S, we conducted transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on both catalysts. The results are shown in Figs. 4, S15, and Table S9. EDX mapping of Ni0.54Mo-A and Ni0.54Mo-S shows uniform distribution of Mo, Ni, and K elements. HRTEM images reveal that Ni0.54Mo-S particles are uniform with an average particle size of approximately 3.24 nm, while Ni0.54Mo-A does not exhibit distinct particles. In Ni0.54Mo-A, clear lattice fringes corresponding to Mo2C (121) and Ni (111) planes are observed, with lattice spacings of 0.227 nm and 0.205 nm, respectively. Mo2C and Ni are in a state of mutual doping, indicating close contact between Mo and Ni [50,51]. The diffraction analysis of the A2 image resulted in the generation of the A4 image, in which the measured diffraction peaks corresponding to Ni(111) and Mo2C(121) were found to be consistent with the lattice spacing analysis of Ni-Mo2C presented in the A2 image. However, some lattice distortion is observed in Figure 4A5, where lattice spacings cannot be measured. We speculate that this lattice distortion may represent the actual catalytic active sites. In Figures 4B2 and B3, the lattice fringes of Mo2C(121) are distinctly visible, with a spacing of 0.228 nm. A diffraction analysis of B4 in B1 reveals prominent diffraction spots corresponding to Ni(111).

Furthermore, we analyzed the relationship between lattice spacings observed in HRTEM images and the corresponding XRD diffraction angles, as shown in Table S9. In Ni0.54Mo-A, the XRD diffraction angles for Mo2C and Ni are 39.54° and 44.15°, corresponding to lattice spacings of 0.2278 nm and 0.2050 nm, respectively. The measured lattice spacings from HRTEM images are 0.227 nm and 0.205 nm, consistent with the XRD results. In Ni0.54Mo-S, the XRD diffraction angle for Mo2C is 39.48°, corresponding to a lattice spacing of 0.2281 nm, which agrees with the measured lattice spacing of 0.228 nm from HRTEM images. This further confirms that the distance between Ni-Mo active sites in Ni0.54Mo-S is closer than that in Ni0.54Mo-A.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption properties of CO, H2, CO2, and NH3 on the catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The CO-TPD curves of the catalysts indicate the presence of both medium and high-intensity adsorption sites for CO, as shown in Figure S16A and Figure S17A. As the Ni/Mo molar ratio increases, the temperature of the CO desorption peaks gradually decreases, suggesting that Ni weakens the adsorption capacity of CO on the catalyst; the CO adsorption capacity increases initially and then decreases, which aligns well with the trend observed in CO conversion rates, indicating that Ni enhances CO conversion [52] Interestingly, the desorption temperature of CO in Ni0.54Mo-S is slightly higher than that in Ni0.54Mo-A. Notably, Ni0.54Mo-S exhibits the highest CO adsorption capacity, as indicated in Table S12, indicating that closer proximity between Mo and Ni active sites favors higher CO adsorption on the catalyst and enhances the CO conversion. Those results are consistent with catalytic performance. The H2-TPD curves reveal that the H2 adsorption sites on the catalyst are predominantly strong, as depicted in Figure S16B and Figure S17B. Meanwhile, the H2 adsorption capacities are relatively low, as shown in Table S12. An appropriate balance between CO and H2 adsorption capacities is advantageous for enhancing CO conversion rates. Furthermore, the CO2-TPD curves indicate the presence of primarily strong basic sites, with a small amount of medium-intensity basic sites, as illustrated in Figure S16C and Figure S17C. On the other hand, the NH3-TPD curves reveal that the Ni0.54Mo-A and Ni0.54Mo-S catalysts are mainly strong acidic sites, with a considerable number of medium-intensity acidic sites, as shown in Figure S16D, Figure S17D and Table S13. The adsorption capacities of Ni0.54Mo-L and Ni0.54Mo-TS are significantly lower than those of Ni0.54Mo-A and Ni0.54Mo-S, likely due to the notably smaller specific surface areas of Ni0.54Mo-L and Ni0.54Mo-TS, as shown in Figures S16, S17 and Table S12, S13. Additionally, we investigated the H2-TPR profiles of Ni0.54Mo-A and Ni0.54Mo-TS,as shown in Figure S18, the comparison reveals that Ni0.54Mo-A contains a greater diversity of oxide species than Ni0.54Mo-TS, which aligns with the XPS analysis of the oxidation states of Mo. This further supports the notion that carbon dots enhance the variety of oxide species on the catalyst surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the distance between Ni-Mo active sites on catalytic performance
ce:section.ce:para: The CO conversion rate, ethanol selectivity, and distribution of C2+OH for Mo-CDs are significantly lower than those of the NiMo bimetallic site catalysts. This is because the Mo-CDs did not form the obvious Mo2C phase. Upon introducing Ni, the XRD patterns show that the NixMo-A catalysts form the obvious Mo2C phase, and the catalyst performance is significantly enhanced. This indicates that in the presence of leaf-derived CDs, Ni is crucial for forming the Mo2C phase. Mixing Mo and Ni source solutions generates nickel molybdate precipitates, resulting in Ni-Mo active sites being spatially close in the catalyst precursor. The high-temperature calcined catalysts inherit this characteristic [53]. Additionally, during the calcination process, hydrogen spillover may occur between Ni and MoO3 [54], which facilitates the formation of the Mo2C phase and the construction of closely contacted Ni-Mo active sites.

The CO conversion rates for Ni0.54Mo-TS, Ni0.54Mo-S, Ni0.54Mo-A, and Ni0.54Mo-L decrease sequentially. This suggests that the distance between Ni-Mo active sites has a good linear relationship with CO conversion. CO-TPD results indicate that the CO adsorption capacity of Ni0.54Mo-S is 1.7 times that of Ni0.54Mo-A, suggesting that smaller distances between Ni-Mo active sites result in greater CO adsorption, thus favoring CO conversion, supporting the viewpoint mentioned above. The C2+OH distributions for Ni0.54Mo-TS, Ni0.54Mo-A, and Ni0.54Mo-L decrease sequentially. This indicates that closer proximity between Ni-Mo active sites facilitates the transfer of reactive intermediates, enhancing carbon chain growth capability, inhibiting methanol formation and increasing the distribution of C2+OH.

Interestingly, although the C2+OH distribution decreases sequentially with the distance increasing, their ethanol selectivity shows the “volcanic” change trend, respectively. The ethanol distribution in the alcohol products also follows this trend. Although the STYROH of Ni0.54Mo-S was higher than that of Ni0.54Mo-A, the STYEtOH of Ni0.54Mo-A is higher than that of Ni0.54Mo-S, indicating that the distance between Ni-Mo active sites in Ni0.54Mo-A is more favorable for ethanol production. In terms of the carbon chain growth probabilities of alcohol products for Ni0.54Mo-TS, Ni0.54Mo-S, Ni0.54Mo-A, and Ni0.54Mo-L, it showed the inverted “volcanic” changes trend, demonstrating that the close contact between Ni-Mo active sites is crucial for the conversion of syngas to C2+OH. In Ni0.54Mo-TS and Ni0.54Mo-S, the smaller distance facilitates the transfer of alkane intermediates and the insertion of adsorbed CO*, promoting carbon chain growth and increasing C2+OH distribution. Meanwhile, the Ni-Mo active sites in physically mixed Ni0.54Mo-L are far apart, lacking atomic-level close contact. Still, the close contact between Ni-Ni or Mo-Mo active sites similarly enhances carbon chain growth capability. Overly close contact between Ni-Mo active sites results in strong carbon chain growth capability, which is not conducive to ethanol formation. However, an appropriate close contact between Ni-Mo is necessary, allowing Ni and Mo atoms to intermix, preventing Ni-Ni and Mo-Mo close contacts, and favoring ethanol production. Therefore, for syngas conversion to ethanol, an optimal carbon chain growth capability is required, corresponding to an appropriate distance between Ni-Mo active sites. The mixing sequence of CDs and Mo, Ni sources with the CDs solution modulates the distance between Ni-Mo active sites. In this study, the Ni0.54Mo-A catalyst shows that the appropriate distance between Ni-Mo active sites is most favorable for syngas conversion to ethanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of carbon dots, solid carbon sources, and gaseous carbon sources
ce:section.ce:para: A comparison of the XRD patterns of Ni0.54Mo-A, Ni0.54Mo-Ac, and Ni0.54Mo-Gas reveals that all three samples exhibit distinct Mo2C phases, with notable shifts in the Ni XRD diffraction peaks. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was employed to examine the morphologies of the three samples, as illustrated in Figure S11. The surface of Ni0.54Mo-A displays a relatively regular block and layered structure, while Ni0.54Mo-Gas presents an irregular block-like structure. In contrast, Ni0.54Mo-Ac shows a clear accumulation of particles within the pores of the activated carbon. Further observations using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on Ni0.54Mo-A (Figures S15A and D) highlight a more pronounced layered structure, with a uniform distribution of Mo and Ni active sites. This uniformity may be attributed to the enhanced anchoring ability of carbon dots for transition metal atoms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In Situ DRIFTS spectra of syngas conversion on the Ni0.54Mo-A, Mo-CDs, and Ni-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To identify key reaction intermediates and elucidate the reaction pathways involved in ethanol synthesis via syngas conversion, we conducted In Situ DRIFTS Spectra of Ni0.54Mo-A, Ni-CDs, and Mo-CDs. The results are depicted in Fig. 5 and S19. Comparative analysis of the gas-phase CO signals at 2113 cm−1 and 2176 cm−1 for Ni0.54Mo-A, Mo-CDs, and Ni-CDs revealed that Mo-CDs exhibited weaker peak intensity. In contrast, Ni-CDs and Ni0.54Mo-A exhibited stronger peak intensities. This observation indicates that introducing Ni significantly enhances the ability of the catalyst to adsorb CO, as shown in Figure S19. In the in situ infrared spectrum of C-CDs as a blank control, the peaks observed at 3430 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 [53,55] correspond to H2O, as shown in Figure S20.

Around 1 minute into the reaction, spectral bands between 2400 and 2300 cm−1 correspond to CO2 from the RWGS [56]. At 4 minutes into the reaction, bands at 2931 cm−1 and 1456 cm−1 are attributed to asymmetric stretching (νas C-H) and asymmetric bending (δas C-H) vibrations of unsaturated C-H bonds (CHx*) [57,58] resulting from the dissociative adsorption of CO (d-CO*) on the catalyst surface. The band at 3031 cm−1 at 25 minutes correspond to the C-H bond in CHO* [57], which may originate from the non-dissociative adsorption of CO* (n-CO*). At 25 minutes into the reaction, a band at 1362 cm−1 attributed to HCOO* appears, and peaks corresponding to HCOO* also appear at 1595 cm−1 and 2852 cm−1 at 55 minutes, indicating the possible presence of HCOO* [57] resulting from (R)WGS. At 55 minutes into the reaction, peaks corresponding to CH3CH2O* appear at 2967 cm−1 and 2575 cm−1, while peaks corresponding to CHx*[57] and CHO* disappear, suggesting that CH3CH2O* may be formed via the insertion of CO*/CHO* into CHx*. However, at 75 minutes, the peaks attributed to CH3CHO* appears at 1575 cm−1 [56] and1437 cm−1 [57]. Since CH3CHO* appears later compared to CH3CH2O* and contains one fewer hydrogen atom, it is unlikely to be formed by inserting CO*/CHO* into CHx*. It is more likely to originate from an alternative pathway. At 65 minutes, bands appearing at 1559 cm−1, 1547 cm−1, and 1532 cm−1 are attributed to CH3COO* [56,57], suggesting that another pathway for syngas conversion to ethanol may involve the coupling of CHx* on the catalyst surface with adsorbed *CO2 or *COOH from the RWGS, forming C-C bonds and subsequently generating CH3COO*, which undergoes progressive hydrogenation to form CH3CHO* and CH3CH2O*, ultimately leading to the formation of CH3CH2OH. This alternative pathway appears to be another important and efficient route for syngas conversion to ethanol and C2+OH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction process of direct conversion of syngas to ethanol on the Ni0.54Mo-a catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the evolution of active species observed in In-Situ-DRIFTS Spectra and other catalyst characterization studies, a possible reaction pathway for syngas conversion to ethanol on the catalyst Ni0.54Mo-A is proposed, as outlined in Scheme 1. Reaction Pathway 1 involves dissociative CO (d-CO*) adsorptionn Ni0 and Mo2+ sites to generate CHx* species [59,60]. Non-dissociative adsorption of CO (n-CO*) occurs on Moδ+ (4≤δ≤6) sites [61], followed by insertion of n-CO* into Metal-CHx* species, leading to C-C bond coupling and formation of CHxCHO*. Subsequent hydrogenation steps result in the formation of CH3CH2OH. Reaction Pathway 2 suggests that the RWGS occurs on Mo2C, generating the intermediate species HCOO*. Alkaline sites stabilize HCOO* and undergo hydrogenation and deoxygenation under the joint action of metal and acidic sites, producing CHO* and CHx*. Insertion of n-CO* or CHO* into Metal-CHx* species leads to the formation of CHxCHO*, which is hydrogenated to produce CH3CH2OH. In Reaction Pathway 3, RWGS occur on Mo2C. Intermediate species such as *COOH or adsorbed *CO2 are stabilized by alkaline or metal sites and undergo carbon-carbon coupling with active species CHx* on metal sites, forming C-C bonds and generating the active species CH3COO*. Subsequent hydrogenation and deoxygenation steps result in the formation of CH3CH2OH.

The active intermediates HCOO*, *COOH, and *CO2 appear to be crucial species in the reaction process. HCOO* can be hydrogenated and deoxygenated stepwise to form active species CHO* and CHx*, which then generate ethanol and C2+OH. However, the active intermediates *COOH and *CO2 seem to play a more significant role. *COOH or *CO2 can directly couple with CHx* to generate intermediates for C2 production [56,62], avoiding the pathway involving n-CO as seen in Pathway 1. In Pathway 1, a large amount of n-CO* leads to the direct hydrogenation of n-CO* to produce more CH3OH. This situation is not present in Pathway 3, possibly explaining why ethanol and C2+OH are more prevalent in the product distribution of this study while CH3OH is less common.

In this study, we propose a strategy for modifying KNiMo2C catalysts with CDs. By adjusting the mixing sequence of the Mo source, Ni source, and CDs solution, we synthesized a series of catalysts with different distances between Ni and Mo active sites. As the distance increased, the selectivity for ethanol exhibited the “volcanic” change trend, with Ni0.54Mo-A achieving the highest ethanol selectivity at 26.2 %, more than twice that of Ni0.54Mo-L. Moreover, the Ni0.54Mo-A catalyst achieved an impressive ethanol distribution of 63.7 %. Additionally, Ni0.54Mo-TS achieved a C2+OH distribution of 93.9 %, and Ni0.54Mo-S had a total STY of 351.6 mg/gcat/h. Characterization results from XRD, XPS, and TEM indicate that the distances between Ni-Mo active sites in Ni0.54Mo-TS, Ni0.54Mo-S, Ni0.54Mo-A, and Ni0.54Mo-L increase sequentially. The overly proximity of Ni-Mo active sites, as well as excessively distant Ni-Mo active sites, are both unfavorable for ethanol production. In Ni0.54Mo-A, the appropriate distance between Ni-Mo active sites can obtain the appropriate carbon chain growth probability, most conducive to enhancing ethanol selectivity and distribution. The RWGS reaction plays significant roles during CO hydrogenation, with HCOO*, *COOH, and CO2 being crucial active species. The direct coupling of CHx with *COOH or *CO2 may be vital to suppressing methanol formation and increasing ethanol selectivity. This study provides an efficient catalyst for syngas-to-ethanol conversion, introduces new research ideas for catalyst design, and offers reasonable insights into the reaction mechanism of syngas-to-ethanol conversion.
